content.text: Azure Resource Manager (ARM) exposes all resource management operations through a single REST API rooted at management.azure.com. ARM REST endpoints are the structured URLs used to address every Azure resource — from subscriptions and resource groups down to individual resources and their child objects. Understanding this address system lets you navigate, script, and automate Azure without relying on higher-level abstractions.

attrs.alt: ARM REST endpoint hierarchy: Tenant → Subscription → Resource Group → Resource Provider → Named Resource

content.text: The ARM Endpoint Root and Scopes
All ARM REST calls share the same base URL: https://management.azure.com. Resources are scoped at four levels, and the URL path expresses that scope precisely.
Create RG, list all resources in an RG
Get, update, delete a specific resource

BASE='https://management.azure.com'
SUB=$(az account show --query id -o tsv)
TOKEN=$(az account get-access-token --resource https://management.azure.com/ --query accessToken -o tsv)

# Tenant level: list all subscriptions
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  "${BASE}/subscriptions?api-version=2022-12-01" | jq '.value[].displayName'

# Subscription level: list all resource groups
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  "${BASE}/subscriptions/${SUB}/resourceGroups?api-version=2021-04-01" | jq '.value[].name'

# Resource Group level: list all resources in a group
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  "${BASE}/subscriptions/${SUB}/resourceGroups/myRG/resources?api-version=2021-04-01" | \
  jq '.value[] | {name:.name, type:.type}'

# Resource level: get a specific VM
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  "${BASE}/subscriptions/${SUB}/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/myVM?api-version=2024-03-01" | \
  jq '{name:.name, size:.properties.hardwareProfile.vmSize, state:.properties.provisioningState}'

Every Azure resource type belongs to a resource provider, identified by a namespace like Microsoft.Compute or Microsoft.Storage. The provider namespace is always part of the resource URL. Resource providers must be registered in a subscription before you can create resources of that type.

# List all registered resource providers in your subscription
az provider list --query "[?registrationState=='Registered'].namespace" -o tsv | sort

# Register a provider (needed before first use)
az provider register --namespace Microsoft.ContainerService

# Check registration state
az provider show --namespace Microsoft.ContainerService --query registrationState

# List all resource types for a provider (with API versions)
az provider show --namespace Microsoft.Compute \
  --query 'resourceTypes[].{type:resourceType, apiVersions:apiVersions[0]}' \
  -o table | head -20

# REST API: list resource providers
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/$SUB/providers?api-version=2021-04-01" | \
  jq '.value[] | select(.registrationState=="Registered") | .namespace' | head -20
ARM supports two types of resources that attach to other resources: child resources (part of the parent's URL path) and extension resources (use a /providers/ segment on any resource as the parent).
A child resource's URL is nested under its parent. The child cannot exist without the parent and is always deleted when the parent is deleted.

# Child resource pattern:
/subscriptions/{id}/resourceGroups/{rg}/providers/{ns}/{type}/{name}/{childType}/{childName}

# Examples:
# SQL Server -> Database
/subscriptions/{id}/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Sql/servers/mySqlServer/databases/myDb

# VM -> VM Extension
/subscriptions/{id}/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/myVM/extensions/AzureMonitorLinuxAgent

# Storage Account -> Blob Container
/subscriptions/{id}/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/myAccount/blobServices/default/containers/myContainer

# Get a SQL database (child of a SQL server)
az rest \
  --method GET \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscriptionId}/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Sql/servers/mySqlServer/databases/myDb?api-version=2022-05-01-preview"

# List VM extensions on a VM
az rest \
  --method GET \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscriptionId}/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/myVM/extensions?api-version=2024-03-01"

# Add a VM extension
az rest \
  --method PUT \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscriptionId}/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/myVM/extensions/CustomScript?api-version=2024-03-01" \
  --body '{
    "location": "eastus",
    "properties": {
      "publisher": "Microsoft.Azure.Extensions",
      "type": "CustomScript",
      "typeHandlerVersion": "2.1",
      "autoUpgradeMinorVersion": true,
      "settings": {"commandToExecute": "echo hello"}
    }
  }'
Extension resources use a /providers/ segment appended to any resource URL, allowing cross-cutting concerns like RBAC, tags, locks, and diagnostics to be applied to any resource.

# Extension resource pattern:
# {parentResourceId}/providers/{extensionNamespace}/{extensionType}/{extensionName}

# RBAC: assign a role to a resource group
az rest \
  --method PUT \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscriptionId}/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/roleAssignments/{roleAssignmentId}?api-version=2022-04-01" \
  --body '{
    "properties": {
      "roleDefinitionId": "/subscriptions/{subscriptionId}/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/roleDefinitions/b24988ac-6180-42a0-ab88-20f7382dd24c",
      "principalId": "{userObjectId}"
    }
  }'

# Resource lock: apply to a specific storage account
az rest \
  --method PUT \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscriptionId}/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/myAccount/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/locks/NoDelete?api-version=2016-09-01" \
  --body '{"properties":{"level":"CanNotDelete"}}'

# Diagnostic settings: enable on a VM
az rest \
  --method GET \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscriptionId}/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/myVM/providers/Microsoft.Insights/diagnosticSettings?api-version=2021-05-01-preview"

In addition to CRUD operations on resources, ARM exposes action endpoints via POST. These are operations that don't map cleanly to create/read/update/delete — like starting a VM, listing storage account keys, or validating a template.
Run a command on a VM

# POST: run a shell command on a Linux VM
az rest \
  --method POST \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscriptionId}/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/myVM/runCommand?api-version=2024-03-01" \
  --body '{
    "commandId": "RunShellScript",
    "script": [
      "df -h",
      "free -m",
      "uptime"
    ]
  }'
# Returns 202 Accepted — poll the Location header for results

# POST: list storage account keys
az rest \
  --method POST \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscriptionId}/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/myAccount/listKeys?api-version=2023-05-01" | \
  jq '.keys[] | {keyName:.keyName, value:.value[:20]}'
Common ARM Endpoint Patterns by Category
ARM endpoints follow consistent patterns within each resource provider. Knowing these patterns lets you predict the endpoint for any resource type.

# Virtual Machines
# GET  /virtualMachines                         → list VMs in RG
# GET  /virtualMachines/{name}                  → get VM
# PUT  /virtualMachines/{name}                  → create/update VM
# DELETE /virtualMachines/{name}               → delete VM
# POST /virtualMachines/{name}/start            → start VM (async)
# POST /virtualMachines/{name}/deallocate       → deallocate (async)
# POST /virtualMachines/{name}/restart          → restart (async)
# GET  /virtualMachines/{name}/instanceView     → power state, extensions

# Example: get VM instance view (power state)
az rest \
  --method GET \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscriptionId}/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/myVM/instanceView?api-version=2024-03-01" | \
  jq '.statuses[] | select(.code | startswith("PowerState")) | .displayStatus'

# Storage Accounts
# GET  /storageAccounts                         → list accounts in RG
# GET  /storageAccounts/{name}                  → get account
# PUT  /storageAccounts/{name}                  → create (async, returns 202)
# PATCH /storageAccounts/{name}                 → update properties
# DELETE /storageAccounts/{name}                → delete
# POST /storageAccounts/{name}/listKeys          → get access keys
# POST /storageAccounts/{name}/regenerateKey     → rotate a key

# Blob Containers (child resources)
# GET  /storageAccounts/{name}/blobServices/default/containers   → list containers
# PUT  /storageAccounts/{name}/blobServices/default/containers/{c} → create container

# Example: list containers in a storage account
az rest \
  --method GET \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscriptionId}/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/myAccount/blobServices/default/containers?api-version=2023-05-01" | \
  jq '.value[].name'

# Virtual Networks
# GET  /virtualNetworks                        → list VNets in RG
# PUT  /virtualNetworks/{name}                 → create/update
# GET  /virtualNetworks/{name}/subnets          → list subnets
# PUT  /virtualNetworks/{name}/subnets/{subnet} → create/update subnet

# Network Security Groups
# GET  /networkSecurityGroups
# PUT  /networkSecurityGroups/{name}
# PUT  /networkSecurityGroups/{name}/securityRules/{rule}

# Example: list all subnets in a VNet
az rest \
  --method GET \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscriptionId}/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworks/myVNet/subnets?api-version=2023-09-01" | \
  jq '.value[] | {name:.name, addressPrefix:.properties.addressPrefix}'

ARM endpoints are subscription-scoped by default but you can make cross-subscription calls by changing the {subscriptionId} segment, provided your token has the necessary RBAC permissions in the target subscription.

# Switch CLI context to a different subscription
az account set --subscription 'other-subscription-id'
TOKEN=$(az account get-access-token --resource https://management.azure.com/ --query accessToken -o tsv)

# Or keep the current token and explicitly specify a different subscription in the URL:
OTHER_SUB='00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000001'
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/${OTHER_SUB}/resourceGroups?api-version=2021-04-01"
# Works if the current token's identity has RBAC access in OTHER_SUB

# For cross-tenant: you need a token issued for the target tenant
# Get a token for a specific tenant:
TOKEN=$(az account get-access-token \
  --resource https://management.azure.com/ \
  --tenant 'other-tenant-id' \
  --query accessToken -o tsv)

# Subscription-level resource list (all types, all groups)
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/${OTHER_SUB}/resources?api-version=2021-04-01" | \
  jq '.value | length'

Deployments are a special ARM resource type under Microsoft.Resources. They track the history of ARM/Bicep template deployments and expose endpoints for creating, listing, validating, and canceling deployments.

# List all deployments in a resource group
az rest \
  --method GET \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscriptionId}/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Resources/deployments?api-version=2022-09-01" | \
  jq '.value[] | {name:.name, state:.properties.provisioningState, timestamp:.properties.timestamp}'

# Validate a deployment (dry run)
az rest \
  --method POST \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscriptionId}/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Resources/deployments/myDeploy/validate?api-version=2022-09-01" \
  --body '{
    "properties": {
      "mode": "Incremental",
      "templateLink": {"uri": "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Azure/azure-quickstart-templates/master/quickstarts/microsoft.storage/storage-account-create/azuredeploy.json"},
      "parameters": {"storageAccountType": {"value": "Standard_LRS"}}
    }
  }'

# Get detailed operation results for a deployment
az rest \
  --method GET \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscriptionId}/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Resources/deployments/myDeploy/operations?api-version=2022-09-01" | \
  jq '.value[] | {op:.operationId, type:.properties.targetResource.resourceType, state:.properties.provisioningState}'

Microsoft publishes the complete ARM REST API reference at learn.microsoft.com/rest/api/azure/. The reference organizes endpoints by resource provider namespace and resource type, showing the URL pattern, supported HTTP methods, request body schema, and response schema for every operation.
Navigate to a resource provider (e.g., Microsoft.Compute) and select a resource type (e.g., Virtual Machines).
Each operation page shows the exact URL, supported api-version values, required request headers, request body schema (JSON), and example responses.
Use the 'Try It' button on the reference page to run API calls directly in the browser against your own subscription.
The 'REST API Browser' at learn.microsoft.com/rest/api/azure/ provides a searchable index of all endpoints across all providers.
az rest --help shows CLI options; combine with the reference docs to construct any call.

# Discover the API version the az CLI uses for any command
az vm list --debug 2>&1 | grep 'api-version'

# Check what resource types a provider has (useful for building URLs)
az provider show --namespace Microsoft.Web \
  --query 'resourceTypes[].{type:resourceType, versions:apiVersions[0]}' \
  -o table

# Get the resource ID of any resource (usable directly in REST calls)
az resource show \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVM \
  --resource-type Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines \
  --query id -o tsv
# Output: /subscriptions/.../resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/myVM

# Use a resource ID directly with az rest
RESOURCE_ID=$(az resource show -g myRG -n myVM --resource-type Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines --query id -o tsv)
az rest --method GET --url "https://management.azure.com${RESOURCE_ID}?api-version=2024-03-01"

Not all ARM operations are scoped to a resource group. Some endpoints operate at the subscription level or even the tenant level, useful for cross-resource-group inventory, subscription management, and policy assignments.

TOKEN=$(az account get-access-token --resource https://management.azure.com/ --query accessToken -o tsv)
SUB=$(az account show --query id -o tsv)

# List all resources across ALL resource groups in a subscription
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/$SUB/resources?api-version=2021-04-01&\$top=100" | \
  jq '.value[] | {name:.name, type:.type, rg: (.id | split("/") | .[4])}'

# Get subscription details
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/$SUB?api-version=2022-12-01" | \
  jq '{name:.displayName, state:.state, tenantId:.tenantId}'

# List resource providers registered in the subscription
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/$SUB/providers?api-version=2021-04-01&\$filter=registrationState+eq+'Registered'" | \
  jq '[.value[].namespace][:10]'

# Tenant level: list ALL subscriptions accessible to the identity
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions?api-version=2022-12-01" | \
  jq '.value[] | {name:.displayName, id:.subscriptionId, state:.state}'

Tags are managed through a dedicated ARM Tags endpoint that supports merge, replace, and delete operations without affecting other resource properties. This is the correct way to add or update tags without overwriting existing ones.

# Get current tags on a resource
RESOURCE_ID=$(az vm show -g myRG -n myVM --query id -o tsv)

az rest \
  --method GET \
  --url "https://management.azure.com${RESOURCE_ID}/providers/Microsoft.Resources/tags/default?api-version=2021-04-01"

# Merge tags (add new tags without removing existing ones)
az rest \
  --method PATCH \
  --url "https://management.azure.com${RESOURCE_ID}/providers/Microsoft.Resources/tags/default?api-version=2021-04-01" \
  --body '{
    "operation": "Merge",
    "properties": {
      "tags": {"env": "prod", "team": "platform", "cost-center": "engineering"}
    }
  }'

# Replace ALL tags on a resource
az rest \
  --method PUT \
  --url "https://management.azure.com${RESOURCE_ID}/providers/Microsoft.Resources/tags/default?api-version=2021-04-01" \
  --body '{
    "properties": {
      "tags": {"env": "prod"}
    }
  }'

# Delete all tags from a resource
az rest \
  --method DELETE \
  --url "https://management.azure.com${RESOURCE_ID}/providers/Microsoft.Resources/tags/default?api-version=2021-04-01"

# Bulk tag all resources in a resource group
az resource list --resource-group myRG --query '[].id' -o tsv | while read ID; do
  az rest --method PATCH \
    --url "https://management.azure.com${ID}/providers/Microsoft.Resources/tags/default?api-version=2021-04-01" \
    --body '{"operation":"Merge","properties":{"tags":{"managed-by":"platform-team"}}}' \
    -o none && echo "Tagged: $ID"
done
Azure Policy is managed through ARM endpoints under Microsoft.Authorization. You can assign, list, and evaluate policy definitions and assignments via REST API.

# List all built-in policy definitions (paginated)
az rest \
  --method GET \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyDefinitions?api-version=2021-06-01&\$filter=policyType+eq+'BuiltIn'" | \
  jq '.value[:5] | .[] | {name:.name, displayName:.properties.displayName}'

# List policy assignments in a subscription
az rest \
  --method GET \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscriptionId}/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyAssignments?api-version=2022-06-01" | \
  jq '.value[] | {name:.name, policy:.properties.policyDefinitionId | split("/") | last}'

# Create a policy assignment (Deny creation of non-eastus resources)
az rest \
  --method PUT \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscriptionId}/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyAssignments/DenyNonEastUS?api-version=2022-06-01" \
  --body '{
    "location": "eastus",
    "properties": {
      "displayName": "Deny non-East US resources",
      "policyDefinitionId": "/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyDefinitions/e56962a6-4747-49cd-b67b-bf8b01975c4c",
      "parameters": {
        "listOfAllowedLocations": {"value": ["eastus", "eastus2"]}
      }
    }
  }'
ARM Activity Log and Metrics Endpoints
ARM exposes endpoints for querying the subscription Activity Log (audit trail of all control-plane operations) and for listing available metrics for any resource.

# Query Activity Log: last 1 hour of write operations
START=$(date -u -d '-1 hour' '+%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ')
END=$(date -u '+%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ')

az rest \
  --method GET \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscriptionId}/providers/Microsoft.Insights/eventtypes/management/values?api-version=2015-04-01&\$filter=eventTimestamp+ge+'${START}'+and+eventTimestamp+le+'${END}'" | \
  jq '.value[] | {
    caller:.caller,
    operation:.operationName.localizedValue,
    resource:.resourceId | split("/") | last,
    status:.status.value,
    time:.eventTimestamp
  }'

# List available metrics for a VM
VM_ID=$(az vm show -g myRG -n myVM --query id -o tsv)
az rest \
  --method GET \
  --url "https://management.azure.com${VM_ID}/providers/Microsoft.Insights/metricDefinitions?api-version=2018-01-01" | \
  jq '.value[] | {name:.name.value, unit:.unit}' | head -20

# Get CPU utilization for a VM (last 1 hour, 5-minute granularity)
az rest \
  --method GET \
  --url "https://management.azure.com${VM_ID}/providers/Microsoft.Insights/metrics?api-version=2018-01-01&metricnames=Percentage+CPU&timespan=${START}/${END}&interval=PT5M" | \
  jq '.value[0].timeseries[0].data[] | {time:.timeStamp, avg:.average}'
When writing scripts or SDK code that targets arbitrary resources, it is useful to construct ARM endpoints from resource IDs. The resource ID itself is the path portion of the ARM URL.

# Get a resource ID from the CLI
RESOURCE_ID=$(az resource show \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVM \
  --resource-type Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines \
  --query id -o tsv)

# Parse segments from the resource ID
SUBSCRIPTION=$(echo $RESOURCE_ID | cut -d'/' -f3)
RESOURCE_GROUP=$(echo $RESOURCE_ID | cut -d'/' -f5)
PROVIDER=$(echo $RESOURCE_ID | cut -d'/' -f7)
RESOURCE_TYPE=$(echo $RESOURCE_ID | cut -d'/' -f8)
RESOURCE_NAME=$(echo $RESOURCE_ID | cut -d'/' -f9)

echo "Subscription: $SUBSCRIPTION"
echo "Resource Group: $RESOURCE_GROUP"
echo "Provider: $PROVIDER"
echo "Type: $RESOURCE_TYPE"
echo "Name: $RESOURCE_NAME"

# Construct the ARM endpoint
API_VERSION='2024-03-01'
ARM_URL="https://management.azure.com${RESOURCE_ID}?api-version=${API_VERSION}"

TOKEN=$(az account get-access-token --resource https://management.azure.com/ --query accessToken -o tsv)
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" "$ARM_URL" | jq .name

from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.mgmt.resource import ResourceManagementClient

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
client = ResourceManagementClient(credential, 'your-subscription-id')

def resource_id_to_arm_url(resource_id: str, api_version: str) -> str:
    return f'https://management.azure.com{resource_id}?api-version={api_version}'

def parse_resource_id(resource_id: str) -> dict:
    parts = resource_id.strip('/').split('/')
    result = {}
    for i in range(0, len(parts) - 1, 2):
        result[parts[i].lower()] = parts[i + 1]
    return result

# Example usage
resource_id = '/subscriptions/abc/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/myVM'
parsed = parse_resource_id(resource_id)
print(parsed)
# {'subscriptions': 'abc', 'resourcegroups': 'myRG', 'providers': 'Microsoft.Compute',
#  'virtualmachines': 'myVM'}

arm_url = resource_id_to_arm_url(resource_id, '2024-03-01')
print(arm_url)

ARM REST endpoints are structured URLs under management.azure.com that address every Azure resource through a consistent hierarchy: tenant → subscription → resource group → resource provider/type → named resource → optional child or extension resources. Every resource provider has its own namespace (Microsoft.Compute, Microsoft.Storage, etc.) and must be registered in a subscription before use. Beyond CRUD operations (GET/PUT/PATCH/DELETE), ARM exposes action endpoints via POST for operations like starting VMs, listing keys, and running commands. Extension resources (via an appended /providers/ segment) allow cross-cutting concerns like RBAC role assignments, resource locks, and diagnostic settings to be applied to any resource without modifying the resource itself. Use az rest with the Azure REST API Reference documentation to call any endpoint not yet wrapped by the CLI or SDK.
